It must have happened gradually, like gaining weight or losing hair—imperceptible day to day until suddenly your pants don't fit and your scalp shows through. All those somedays piled up in storage somewhere, growing mold.

The honey woman was right, though I'm not sure about what. Maybe bees know that sweetness takes time and collective effort. Maybe they know that individual sacrifice serves something larger. Or maybe they just know to dance when they find something good, to share the coordinates with whoever's listening.

Last week I googled "how to be happy" at 2 AM and felt simultaneously like the saddest and most ridiculous person alive. The internet offered 73 million results in 0.4 seconds. I didn't click any of them. Instead, I ate cereal in bed and watched videos of people restoring old furniture until the sun came up. There's something hopeful about taking something broken and making it functional again, even if it's someone else doing it to someone else's chair.

The truth is, most days feel like rough drafts of better days I might have later. But lately I'm wondering if the rough draft is all we get—if waiting for the final version means missing the crossed-out words and margin notes that might matter more than whatever clean copy we're hoping for.

Tomorrow I'll buy more honey. The old woman will tell me the bees know, and I'll nod like I understand. Maybe understanding isn't the point. Maybe the point is showing up, exchanging currency for sweetness, believing there's wisdom in the gathering of small things. Maybe the bees know that too.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old man at the corner table orders the same thing every morning: black coffee, wheat toast, scrambled eggs. No butter. He reads a paperback while he eats, never looking up when the bell above the door chimes. I've been watching him for three weeks now, though I'm not sure why.

Maybe it's because he reminds me of my grandfather, who died before I could form real memories of him. I have fragments—the smell of pipe tobacco, rough hands lifting me toward a ceiling that seemed impossibly high, a voice like gravel singing something in Italian. Or maybe Portuguese. Memory is unreliable that way, filling gaps with wishful thinking.

The diner itself feels suspended in time. Red vinyl booths patched with duct tape, a jukebox that hasn't worked since the Clinton administration, fluorescent lights that hum like dying insects. The coffee is terrible, but it's hot and cheap, and sometimes that's all you need.

I started coming here after Sarah left. Not immediately after—first there were weeks of lying in bed, staring at the water stain on the ceiling that looked like a map of some undiscovered continent. Then weeks of frantic activity: repainting the apartment, joining a gym, downloading dating apps I'd delete hours later. But eventually, I needed a place that didn't know my story, where I could sit and be nobody in particular.

The waitress, Dolores, has stopped asking if I want anything besides coffee. She just refills my mug and moves on, efficient in the way of people who've learned not to waste motion or words. There's something comforting about being known without being known, if that makes sense.

Today, the old man's book is different. For weeks it's been the same worn copy of something with a submarine on the cover. But now he's reading something with yellow pages and a cracked spine that looks even older. He adjusts his glasses, holds the book at arm's length, then brings it closer. The dance of aging eyes.

Outside, it's starting to rain. Not a dramatic storm, just a steady October drizzle that turns the world watercolor-soft. A woman hurries past, newspaper held over her head, and I think about how we're all just trying to stay dry in one way or another.

My phone buzzes. Mom, probably, checking in again. She's been doing that more since Sarah left, careful little texts that dance around the topic. "How are you?" means "Are you eating?" means "I'm worried" means "I love you." I'll respond later with my own code: "Fine, just busy" means "Still breathing" means "Please don't worry" means "I love you too."

The old man closes his book, leaves exact change plus a precise fifteen percent tip, and stands to go. But then he does something new—he looks directly at me and nods. Just once, a small acknowledgment that makes my chest tighten unexpectedly.

After he leaves, I move to his table. It still smells faintly of eggs and the particular old-person scent that's part mothballs, part medicine, part something indefinable but not unpleasant. There's a bookmark on the table, forgotten. It's a playing card—the seven of hearts—worn soft at the edges.

I could run after him, return it. But I don't. Instead, I pocket it, this small token of someone else's life. Not theft, exactly. More like finding a message in a bottle, even if the message is just "I was here, I read books, I marked my place."

Dolores comes over with the coffee pot. "You know him?" she asks, first words she's spoken to me beyond "More coffee?"

"No," I say. "Just familiar strangers."

She nods like this makes perfect sense, and maybe it does. "He's been coming here forty years. Wife died last spring. Reads her books now." She taps the table where the book was. "Romance novels, mostly. Said he never understood what she saw in them, but now..." She shrugs, moves on to the next table.

The rain has stopped. Sun breaks through the clouds in that dramatic way that makes everything look staged, too beautiful to be real. The wet street shimmers like it's paved with broken mirrors.

I think about the old man going home to his empty apartment, reading his dead wife's books, trying to understand something that can't be understood, only felt. I think about Sarah, wherever she is, maybe sitting in a different diner, maybe happy, maybe not. I think about my grandfather, who may or may not have sung to me in a language I'll never identify.

The seven of hearts feels warm in my pocket. In card reading, hearts mean love, emotions, connections. Seven means assessment, reflection, a pause before moving forward. Or maybe it just means someone needed to keep their place in a story.

Tomorrow I'll come back. The old man will order his usual. We might nod at each other again, or we might not. But I'll be here, and he'll be here, and Dolores will pour terrible coffee, and the broken jukebox will stay silent, and life will continue its strange, mundane, heartbreaking march forward.

Sometimes that's enough. Sometimes that's everything.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old woman at the farmers market sold me lemons that weren't quite ripe, their skins still tinged with green at the stems. "They'll be perfect in three days," she promised, pressing the paper bag into my hands with fingers that reminded me of tree roots—gnarled, strong, necessary. I believed her because she had that particular kind of certainty that comes from knowing things through repetition rather than theory.

Walking home, I thought about expertise, how it accumulates in the body like sediment. My grandmother could tell you the weather was changing by the way her knee clicked. My daughter knows exactly when her baby is about to cry, some invisible shift in the air pressure around his small body that only she can detect. We are all barometers of our own specific worlds.

The lemons sat on my counter for exactly three days. On the third morning, their smell had changed—brighter, almost aggressive in its citrus announcement. The green had vanished like a bruise healing in reverse. I cut one open and the juice ran everywhere, more than seemed possible for such a modest fruit. The old woman had been right, of course. 

There's something humbling about being bad at waiting. Modern life has trained us out of it—everything arrives tomorrow, streams instantly, updates in real-time. But some things resist this compression. Bread must rise. Seasons must turn. Children must grow at their own pace, no matter how desperately we want to fast-forward through the sleepless nights or rewind to the sweet weight of them as newborns.

I made lemon bars with those perfect lemons, following my mother's recipe which was really her mother's recipe which was probably someone else's recipe before that, transformed by each generation's particular mistakes and improvisations. My mother always added too much butter. I never sift the flour despite the instructions explicitly saying to sift. My daughter will probably make them with some flour substitute that hasn't been invented yet, and they'll still be called Grandma's lemon bars.

The physics of baking is unforgiving—temperature, ratio, timing all matter in ways that feel almost moral. You cannot negotiate with gluten development. You cannot convince sugar to cream with butter any faster than it wants to. There's a democracy to it that I find comforting: even the wealthy and powerful cannot make their bread rise faster without ruining it.

While the bars baked, I sat in my backyard and watched the light change. It was that particular quality of afternoon where everything looks like it's been painted with honey—even the chain-link fence, even the recycling bins. The neighbor's cat walked along the fence with impossible precision, placing each paw exactly where it needed to be without looking. I envied its certainty.

My phone buzzed with seventeen different notifications, each one marked urgent by someone who had forgotten what that word actually meant. I left it face down on the grass and watched clouds form and reform, that slow-motion choreography that happens whether we witness it or not. Sometimes I wonder if we've confused documenting life with living it. Every sunset photographed, every meal shared, every thought transmitted. As if experience that isn't broadcast doesn't quite count.

The timer went off, pulling me back inside. The bars had set perfectly, that just-firm custard consistency that hovers between liquid and solid, a state of matter that doesn't have a proper name but that everyone recognizes when they taste it. I cut them into squares while still warm, which you're not supposed to do, but I've learned that some rules are more like suggestions, and knowing which is which might be the only wisdom that matters.

I brought a plate to the old woman at the market the next week. She bit into one thoughtfully, her eyes closing for a moment. "You didn't sift the flour," she said, not unkindly. I asked how she could tell. "Sixty years of making mistakes," she replied, then took another bite. "But sometimes the mistakes make them better."

We stood there in comfortable silence, two women eating lemon bars at seven in the morning, surrounded by pyramids of tomatoes and bundles of kale that looked like bouquets for vegetarians. A child ran past, chasing a dog, or maybe the dog was chasing the child—it was hard to tell where one joy ended and the other began.

The old woman wrapped up some peaches for me, their fuzz like the softest suede. "Four days," she said, and I nodded, already imagining their transformation, that alchemy of time and temperature that turns potential into sweetness. I paid her in cash, bills that had been folded and refolded so many times they felt like cloth.

Walking home, I thought about cycles—how we measure our lives in returns and repetitions. The market every Saturday. The seasons. The way certain stories get told at every family gathering until they become more true than what actually happened. There's a comfort in these rhythms, a music we move to without consciously hearing it.

At home, I placed the peaches on the counter next to a bowl of lemons I'd bought three days ago, now perfectly ripe and waiting. Through the window, I could see the neighbor's cat in the same spot on the fence, the light once again turning everything golden, the world continuing its ancient patterns while we small humans try to make sense of our brief participation in it all.

The peaches would be perfect in four days. I marked it on my calendar, that small act of faith in the future, in the old woman's knowledge, in the simple patience of fruit becoming itself. Some things cannot be rushed. Thank goodness for that.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old woman at the farmer's market sells honey in mason jars, and every Saturday she tells me the same story about her bees. How they know her footsteps. How they mourn when one of their sisters dies. I buy a jar each week, not because I need that much honey, but because I need to hear someone talk about loving something that could hurt them.

My kitchen cabinet is full of amber jars now, catching light like captured sunset. Sometimes I open them just to smell summer – that thick sweetness that speaks of flowers I'll never see, fields I'll never walk through. The bees traveled miles to make this. The old woman's crooked fingers labeled each jar with the month and year, as if time could be preserved in sweetness.

I've been thinking about preservation lately. How we're all trying to save something. My neighbor presses flowers between dictionary pages. She showed me once – violet pansies flattened between "melancholy" and "memory." She says she's making bookmarks for her grandchildren, but they never visit. The flowers accumulate like silent words.

There's a certain kind of loneliness that comes from keeping things. Museum loneliness. Archive loneliness. The careful distance between the thing preserved and the life it once held. I think about this while spreading honey on toast, wondering if the bees remember the flowers, if sweetness is its own kind of memory.

My mother called yesterday. She's cleaning out the attic, finding boxes of my childhood drawings. "Should I keep them?" she asked, and I could hear the weight of thirty years in her voice. The weight of refrigerator art and handprint turkeys, crayon suns with stick-figure families beneath them. "I don't know," I told her, because I don't know if love means holding on or letting go.

The truth is, I'm afraid of both. Afraid of empty hands and afraid of hands too full to hold anything new. The honey jars multiply. The dictionary grows thick with pressed flowers. My mother's attic holds the child I was, suspended in construction paper and glitter glue.

But here's what the bee woman doesn't say, what I've learned from watching her weathered hands move among the jars: the bees don't make honey for us. They make it for winter, for survival, for reasons that have nothing to do with our need to sweeten things. We're just lucky observers, careful thieves of their industry.

Maybe that's what all preservation is – a kind of beautiful theft. We take the flower from the field, the moment from time's stream, the honey from the hive. We label it, date it, put it behind glass or between pages. We say we're saving it, but really we're just trying to save ourselves from the passing of things.

Last week, the bee woman wasn't at her usual stall. Her nephew was there instead, selling the same honey in the same mason jars. "She's with her bees," he said, which could mean anything. Could mean tending them. Could mean becoming earth that feeds the flowers that feed the bees that make the honey that fills the jars that line my cabinet like a prayer to permanence.

I bought three jars from him. He didn't tell me any stories.

Walking home, I passed the cemetery where stones mark attempts at permanent memory. Names and dates carved deep, but even stone erodes. Even granite forgets. There were fresh flowers on some graves, plastic flowers on others. The plastic ones last longer but mean less, or maybe they mean more – the peculiar devotion of choosing what endures over what's real.

I want to call my mother back. Tell her to throw it all away. Tell her to keep every scrap. Tell her that love isn't measured in what we keep but in what we're willing to carry. But I don't call. Instead, I open a jar from last summer, spread honey on warm bread, and taste time itself – the mornings the bees flew out, the afternoons they returned heavy with nectar, the nights they fanned their wings to thicken sweetness.

Tomorrow is Saturday. If the bee woman is back, I'll buy another jar. If she isn't, I'll buy one anyway. Her nephew doesn't need to know that my cabinet is full, that I've started giving jars away to strangers, pressing honey into their hands like sharing a secret about the world.

Here's the secret: everything we love will leave us, is leaving us, has already left. The bees die after they sting, defending sweetness. The flowers pressed in dictionaries were dead the moment they were picked. My mother's attic holds a child who doesn't exist anymore.

But here's the other secret: the honey remains sweet. The pressed flowers still hold their color. The child in those drawings was real, is real, will always be real in the way that past things are real – not present but persistent, gone but glowing, like light from dead stars still reaching us, still bright, still guiding us home.

The honey catches morning light through my window. Amber philosophy, golden archaeology. Each jar a small world, complete and contained.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old lighthouse keeper told me once that the sea remembers everything. I didn't believe him then—I was nineteen and working the summer at my uncle's seafood restaurant, more interested in the tips from tourists than the ramblings of a man who'd spent forty years watching waves. But now, standing at the edge of the pier with salt spray on my face, I think maybe he was right.

Memory is strange. It doesn't follow rules. Sometimes the most important moments slip through your fingers like water, while trivial Tuesday afternoons from decades past sit in your mind, perfectly preserved like insects in amber. I can't remember my grandmother's voice, but I can recall exactly how the linoleum felt under my bare feet in her kitchen, that specific coolness on July mornings before the heat set in.

The human brain contains roughly 86 billion neurons, each one capable of forming thousands of connections. We are walking libraries of experience, every moment theoretically retrievable, yet most of it lost in the vast archive of consciousness. What determines what we keep? What we lose?

My daughter asked me yesterday if I remembered being her age—seven, gap-toothed, obsessed with dolphins. I told her yes, but it was only partially true. I remember fragments: the weight of my school backpack, the smell of fresh crayons, the paralyzing fear of the neighbor's German Shepherd. But I don't remember what it felt like to believe in infinite possibility, to wake up without the weight of mortgage payments and climate change and the peculiar exhaustion of modern adulthood.

There's a Japanese word—komorebi—that describes sunlight filtering through leaves. No direct English translation exists, which seems like evidence that some experiences are so specific, so necessary, that entire cultures build language around them. What words are we missing? What experiences go unnamed and therefore unnoticed?

I've been thinking about attention lately, how it shapes reality. The security camera footage of our lives would show a completely different story than the one we remember. It would capture all the moments we weren't paying attention—the background conversations, the strangers who passed us, the birds that flew overhead while we stared at our phones. We are each the unreliable narrator of our own existence.

My mother is losing her memory now. Alzheimer's, they say, though the word feels too clinical for what's happening. She's not just forgetting; she's traveling through time. Yesterday she asked me to help her study for a biology exam she took in 1963. Last week, she was worried about missing her wedding, which happened before I was born. She lives in a shuffle of decades, unstuck from linear time. Sometimes I envy her freedom from chronology, even as it breaks my heart.

The lighthouse keeper—his name was Edmund—used to say that every seventh wave was larger than the rest. It's a myth, technically. Wave patterns are far more complex than that. But humans need patterns, need meaning, need to believe in some underlying order. So we count to seven, we wish on stars, we hold our breath through tunnels.

I've started writing things down more. Not the big things—those seem to take care of themselves. But the small ones: the way my coffee mug feels in my hands on cold mornings, the specific shade of green the maple tree turns before all the others, my son's laugh when he's genuinely surprised versus when he's being polite. These seem like the things most likely to disappear, and therefore the most important to preserve.

There's a theory in physics about the conservation of information—that information, like energy, cannot be destroyed. Every bit of data about every particle that has ever existed is somehow preserved in the universe. If that's true, then somewhere, encoded in the fabric of spacetime, is the afternoon my father taught me to ride a bike, the first time I saw the ocean, every conversation I've ever had. We are not lost; we are simply scattered.

But theory is cold comfort at 3 AM when you're trying to remember the last thing someone said to you, when you're searching your memory for clues you should have noticed, for words you should have said. The past is a foreign country, but we're all refugees from it, carrying whatever we could save.

Edmund died last winter. I went to his funeral, surprised by how many people came. The church was full of strangers who'd watched his light from their boats, who'd been guided home by his vigilance. His daughter spoke about how he'd recorded the weather every single day for forty years, notebooks full of wind speed and barometric pressure and cloud formations. She said she didn't know what to do with them all.

Keep them, I wanted to tell her. Keep everything. You never know what will matter until suddenly it's the only thing that does. One day you'll want to know what the weather was like on some random Tuesday, and those notebooks will be a door back to a world where your father was still watching the sky, still keeping track, still here.

The sea remembers everything, Edmund told me. Maybe that's why we're drawn to it. Standing at its edge, we sense that vast repository of time, all those waves that have touched other shores, carrying messages we'll never decode. The sea remembers, and perhaps that's enough—to know that somewhere, something holds the whole story, even if we only get to read our own small chapter.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old woman at the corner table orders the same thing every morning: black coffee, wheat toast, no butter. She spreads out the newspaper like a map of the world, smoothing the creases with arthritic hands that once played Chopin at Carnegie Hall. Nobody here knows this. The barista calls her "hon" and doesn't notice how her fingers still move in phantom arpeggios against the wooden table.

I'm watching her again, pretending to work on my laptop. The screen's been dark for twenty minutes. Instead, I'm cataloging the small miracles of Tuesday morning: steam rising from cups like answered prayers, the door chime playing its two-note song of arrival and departure, the way light catches the dust motes and turns them into something worth noticing.

My therapist says I dissociate. I prefer to think I'm just paying attention to the wrong things. Or the right things. Who decides what deserves our focus? The businessman shouting into his phone about quarterly projections doesn't see the child at the next table building a tower from sugar packets, engineering joy from nothing. The tower falls. The child laughs. The businessman frowns at the noise. This is the entire human condition in miniature.

The coffee here is terrible, honestly. Burnt and bitter, like disappointment you can drink. But I come anyway because perfect coffee would ruin everything. We need the flaws. They're the cracks where the light gets in, where the stories seep through.

A couple in the corner is breaking up. She's doing it gently, with words like "timing" and "different paths." He's stirring his coffee counterclockwise, as if he could reverse time. I want to tell him that I tried that once, that it doesn't work, that moving forward is the only magic we get. But strangers don't want your wisdom. They want to discover their own mistakes, earn their own scars.

The old woman turns a page. Her wedding ring catches the light. I wonder if her husband is dead or just sleeping late, if Chopin still sounds the same in her dreams, if she misses the weight of a piano beneath her hands. We carry so many ghosts. They sit with us at corner tables, order black coffee, read over our shoulders.

My phone buzzes. A text from my mother: "Just checking in." These three words contain multitudes – worry, love, the peculiar gravity of family. I type back: "I'm good." This is both true and not true, the way most important things are.

Outside, the city is waking up angry. Horns and sirens and the metal screech of buses stopping. But in here, we've created this temporary peace, this agreement to be quiet together. The barista starts the espresso machine, and it sounds like a tiny dragon clearing its throat. Someone laughs at something on their phone. These sounds are a kind of music, improvised and imperfect.

I think about the theory that every choice creates a parallel universe. Somewhere, there's a version of me who became a doctor like my father wanted. Another who stayed in Portland with Sarah. Another who never learned to love coffee shops, who doesn't know this specific peace of being alone but not lonely, surrounded by strangers who ask nothing of you but to share space.

The old woman stands to leave. She folds her newspaper precisely, leaves a two-dollar tip. As she passes my table, she stops. "You're a writer," she says. Not a question.

"How did you know?"

She smiles. "You watch like someone trying to remember everything."

"I'm trying," I say.

"That's all anyone can do." She pulls on her coat. "I was a pianist, you know. A long time ago."

"I know," I admit.

She nods, unsurprised. "Writers always know. It's very annoying." But she's still smiling as she leaves.

The space she leaves behind feels important somehow. The businessman is gone too, replaced by a student with highlighted textbooks and the specific exhaustion of finals week. The child has moved on from sugar packets to napkin origami. The broken-up couple sits in silence now, but it's not uncomfortable. Sometimes we need witnesses to our endings.

I open my laptop again. The blank document stares at me like a dare. I think about the old woman's hands, about phantom music, about the way we all carry our past selves like matryoshka dolls, nested inside us. I think about my mother checking in, about Sarah in Portland probably drinking better coffee, about the doctor I never became saving no one's life but maybe my own by choosing this instead.

The cursor blinks. I start typing: "The old woman at the corner table orders the same thing every morning."

Because this is what we do. We watch. We witness. We try to catch the fleeting moments before they dissolve like sugar in hot coffee. We fail mostly, but sometimes – sometimes we get it right. Sometimes we preserve a Tuesday morning, a corner table, an old woman who once played Chopin, and in doing so, we prove that it all mattered.

The coffee is still terrible. The light is still perfect. I'm still here, trying to remember everything.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old lighthouse keeper told me once that the sea remembers everything. I didn't believe him then, standing on those salt-worn rocks with the Atlantic spray misting my face, but now I'm not so sure. Memory, after all, is just another kind of tide—pulling away, rushing back, leaving things behind on the shore that you forgot you'd lost.

I've been thinking about permanence lately. About how we build these elaborate sandcastles of meaning, knowing full well the water's coming. My daughter asks me why we bother making her bed every morning when she's just going to mess it up again at night, and I don't have a good answer except that maybe the making and unmaking is the whole point. Maybe Sisyphus was actually the happiest man alive.

There's a particular quality to 4 AM silence that doesn't exist at any other hour. It's not the dead quiet of midnight or the expectant hush of dawn—it's something else entirely, like the world is holding its breath between heartbeats. I've been waking up at that exact time for three weeks now, not by choice but by some internal timer I can't seem to reset. My cat notices. She pads over and settles on my chest, purring like she's trying to restart my engine.

The truth is, I've been procrastinating calling my brother. It's been six months since our last conversation, which ended with him saying "I just don't know who you are anymore," and me having no response because, honestly, neither do I. Identity seems like such a fluid thing these days, sloshing around in whatever container I pour myself into. Worker. Sister. Neighbor who always forgets to bring her trash bins in. Night wanderer. Keeper of too many houseplants.

Speaking of plants: I finally figured out why my monstera was dying. Too much love, it turns out. I was watering it every few days, rotating it for even light, misting its leaves like some kind of botanical helicopter parent. The thing needed neglect. It needed to struggle a little, to send its roots searching. There's probably a metaphor there, but I'm tired of turning everything into a lesson. Sometimes a plant is just a plant, and sometimes you're just bad at keeping things alive.

My neighbor's wind chimes play minor sevenths in the breeze. I looked it up—that interval, the distance between those two notes—and learned it's called "the devil's interval" in medieval music theory. Monks weren't allowed to sing it. Too dissonant, too unsettling, too likely to let the devil in through the cracks in their devotion. But here it is, tinkling away in suburban America, and nobody's possessed yet. Or maybe we all are, and that's why everything feels slightly off-key.

I keep a running list of things I want to remember: the way coffee steam looks like ghost handwriting in the morning light. How my mother used to test if spaghetti was done by throwing it at the wall. The exact shade of blue the sky turns just before a thunderstorm. That couple I saw at the grocery store, both in their eighties, still holding hands while debating which cereal to buy. These feel important somehow, though I can't say why.

The lighthouse keeper died last winter. I read about it in the paper, a small notice tucked between the tide tables and an ad for boat insurance. They said he was survived by no one, but that's not exactly true. He's survived by everyone who ever heard his stories about ships that never came back, about lights that stayed on through impossible storms, about the sea remembering everything.

There's this moment right before you fall asleep where thoughts become unmoored from logic, where you might suddenly believe you can taste colors or that your childhood home is secretly underwater. Scientists call it the hypnagogic state. I call it the truth leaking in. Because maybe the rational world we've constructed is the real dream, and everything else—the nonsense, the contradictions, the impossible feelings—maybe that's what's actually real.

I should mention that none of this is what I meant to write. I sat down intending to make a grocery list, but here we are, a thousand words later, and I still don't have milk. This happens sometimes—the mundane transforms into something else entirely, like those fairy tales where you set out to buy bread and come back with magic beans. Except there's no giant's castle at the end of this, just me, realizing that I've been avoiding the grocery store because I might run into someone I used to love.

The sea remembers everything. The lighthouse keeper told me that, and then he told me something else: it remembers, but it doesn't mourn. It just keeps moving, tide after tide, carrying things away and bringing them back transformed. Salt-smoothed glass that was once someone's beer bottle. Driftwood that was once someone's fence. Memory without sentiment, history without nostalgia.

Maybe that's what I need to learn. How to remember without drowning in it. How to let the tide pull away what needs to go and trust that whatever comes back was meant to stay. How to wake at 4 AM and just listen to the silence without trying to fill it with meaning.

Tomorrow, I'll call my brother. Or I won't. Either way, the world keeps turning, the cat keeps purring, and somewhere, the sea keeps remembering everything while pretending to forget.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old woman at the farmer's market sells honey in mason jars with handwritten labels. Every Saturday, she's there by seven, arranging them on a checkered tablecloth—wildflower, clover, orange blossom—each one catching the morning light like trapped sunshine. I've been buying from her for three years now, and we've never exchanged more than prices and pleasantries, but I know she lost her husband last spring because the labels changed from "Henry's Honey" to just "Honey."

Small things tell big stories if you're paying attention.

I've been thinking about attention lately, how it's become our scarcest resource. Not time—we've always been short on time. But attention is different. It's the quality of presence we bring to our moments, and most of us are bankrupt. We scroll through our phones at red lights, half-listen to our children's stories while checking email, eat lunch at our desks without tasting a single bite. We're everywhere and nowhere, ghosts haunting our own lives.

Yesterday, I watched a child discover her shadow. She was maybe three, toddling across the park, when suddenly she noticed this dark companion moving with her. She stopped. Stepped forward. Stepped back. Raised her arms like a tiny conductor, watched her shadow do the same. For five full minutes, she was completely absorbed in this dialogue with herself, this dance with light and form. Her mother sat on a bench, also watching, also smiling, no phone in sight. Both of them fully there, fully present to wonder.

When did we stop noticing shadows?

There's a Japanese concept called "komorebi"—the interplay of sunlight through leaves. They have a word for this specific quality of light, this exact phenomenon that happens billions of times every day but rarely makes us pause. The Japanese have words for lots of things we don't: the sadness of seeing a stuffed animal left behind, the way someone looks when they think no one is watching, the bittersweetness of a brief, beautiful moment.

Maybe having words for things helps us see them. Or maybe seeing them clearly enough compels us to name them.

I'm writing this by hand in a coffee shop that's about to close. The barista is wiping down tables with the practiced efficiency of someone who's done this a thousand times. The couple in the corner is having what looks like a first date—lots of nervous laughter, careful body language. Outside, October is doing what October does: painting everything gold before the gray arrives. A man walks by with his dog, both of them old, both of them moving with the same unhurried rhythm that says they've walked this route for years.

All of this is happening. All of this is ordinary. All of this is irreplaceable.

We talk about saving time, spending time, wasting time, as if time were currency. But time isn't money—it's more like weather. It happens to us, around us, through us. We can prepare for it, dress for it, sometimes predict it, but we can't save it in bottles like honey. We can only be in it, fully or not at all.

The honey woman knows this, I think. She sits at her booth, not on her phone, not reading, just watching people pass. Sometimes she holds a jar up to the sun, turning it slowly, examining it like a jeweler with a precious stone. She knows every batch is different—different flowers, different weather, different bees with their own mysterious preferences. She can probably taste the variations that I'm too rushed to notice: the difference between honey made in May versus July, between hives near the lavender field and those by the blackberry brambles.

Expertise is really just deep attention paid over time.

My grandmother used to say she could predict rain by the way her joints ached, the way the sparrows gathered, the particular green tint the sky took on. We thought it was superstition until we learned that birds can sense barometric pressure changes, that human joints really do respond to weather shifts, that the sky does change color before storms. She wasn't mystical; she was observant. She paid attention to her world with the dedication of a scientist and the patience of a poet.

I wonder what we're missing now, in our climate-controlled, notification-filled, artificially-lit worlds. What subtle signals are we too distracted to receive? What patterns are revealing themselves to no one?

The coffee shop is playing that song about how we're all just somebody that somebody used to know. The barista turns the sign from "Open" to "Closed." The first-date couple exchanges numbers, thumbs flying across screens with the urgency of the newly smitten. The October light is doing that thing where it makes everything look like a memory even while it's still happening.

Tomorrow I'll go back to the farmer's market. I'll buy honey from the old woman, and maybe this time I'll tell her I'm sorry about Henry. Or maybe I won't. Maybe it's enough to know, to notice, to let that knowledge season my sweetness with something deeper. Maybe attention itself is a form of love—quiet, unannounced, but real.

The child who discovered her shadow is probably asleep now, dreaming whatever dreams come to those who still see magic in the ordinary. Somewhere, bees are sleeping too, clustered together in the dark architecture of their hives, their summer's work crystallizing into gold.

Small things telling their big stories, if anyone's still listening.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old woman at the farmers market told me that tomatoes remember everything. She said it while weighing my heirlooms on a scale that looked like it had survived two world wars, her spotted hands steady despite their tremor. "They remember the soil, the rain, who planted them. Even remember being seeds." I smiled politely, the way you do when strangers offer unsolicited philosophy with your produce.

But driving home, I couldn't stop thinking about it. What if memory isn't unique to brains? What if it's just information, encoded everywhere – in the rings of trees, the layers of rock, the DNA of everything that lives and has lived? 

My daughter called while I was unpacking the groceries. She's thirty-two now, lives in Portland, designs sustainable packaging for a company I can never remember the name of. Something with "green" in it. Or "leaf." She wanted to know if I was coming for Thanksgiving.

"Of course," I said, though we both knew I'd been saying that for three years running and hadn't made it once. First it was Covid, then Dad's surgery, then the excuse I can't even remember now. The truth is simpler: her apartment smells like patchouli and her husband talks too much about cryptocurrency.

But this year feels different. This year I wake up at 3 AM and count backwards, trying to remember when I last felt young. Not young-young, but that middle kind of young where your knees don't hurt and the future still feels infinite. Forty-seven? Fifty-two? The numbers blur together like highway markers on a long drive.

The tomatoes sit on my counter, red and accusatory. I slice one open – its flesh is marbled like expensive steak, seeds suspended in gel like insects in amber. If this tomato remembers everything, does it remember being eaten? Does it know what's coming?

I think about calling my daughter back, telling her about the tomato woman. She'd laugh, but not meanly. She'd say something about quantum consciousness or mycological networks, references to documentaries I should watch, books I should read. She inherited her father's need to explain mystery away, to trap wonder in words until it suffocates.

Instead, I eat the tomato with just salt. It tastes like summer, though it's October now and the leaves outside my window are burning gold. 

There's this moment, right before dawn, when the world feels hollow. Not empty – hollow. Like it's waiting to be filled. I've been waking up in that moment for months now, lying there until the feeling passes, until the day rushes in with its appointments and obligations, its small betrayals and smaller victories.

My mother had insomnia too, near the end. She'd wander the house at night, reorganizing closets, writing letters she'd never send. I found them after she died: letters to her elementary school teacher, to the boy who kissed her at the 1959 county fair, to me at ages I'd already passed. "Dear Susan at sixteen," one began, "don't let them cut your hair. You'll regret it forever."

I did let them cut it. I did regret it. Not forever, but for long enough that it felt like prophecy.

The farmers market woman had silver hair down to her waist, braided like rope. When she handed me my change, her fingers brushed mine and they were surprisingly warm, almost fevered. "Plant something," she said. "Anything. But plant it soon."

I wanted to tell her that I kill everything I plant, that I have what my daughter calls "the opposite of a green thumb." But she'd already moved on to the next customer, weighing out green beans with the same careful attention.

Tomorrow I'll drive to the garden center. I'll buy bulbs – tulips probably, or maybe daffodils. Something that knows how to sleep through winter and remember spring. I'll dig holes in the cold ground, my knees creaking, my back complaining. I'll plant them deep, cover them with soil and mulch, and then I'll forget where I put them.

But they'll remember. They'll remember everything – the depth of the darkness, the weight of the snow, the exact moment when the sun's angle whispers "now." They'll push through in April, maybe May, green shoots breaking earth like tiny resurrections.

My daughter will visit in spring. She always does. She'll see the flowers and say, "Mom, these are beautiful. When did you plant them?" And I'll lie. I'll say I can't remember, that they must have been there all along, volunteers from some previous owner's garden.

But the truth will be simpler: I planted them in October, when a stranger told me that tomatoes remember everything, when I couldn't sleep for thinking about time passing, when the hollowness before dawn felt too vast to fill with anything but hope.

The bulbs will remember. They'll remember my hands in the soil, the sound of birds overhead, the way I stood up too fast and had to catch myself against the fence. They'll remember being seeds in their own way – not seeds exactly, but possibilities wrapped in papery skin, waiting.

Everything remembers. Everything waits. Everything breaks through eventually.

Even us.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old lighthouse keeper told me once that the sea remembers everything. I didn't believe him then, standing in his cluttered kitchen while rain hammered the windows, watching him pour tea with hands that trembled not from age but from something else entirely. Now, thirty years later, I understand what he meant.

Memory isn't just something we carry in our heads. It lives in places, in objects, in the space between words. My grandmother's garden still remembers her, even though she's been gone for a decade. The tomatoes grow exactly where she planted them, returning each summer like clockwork, their seeds carrying the blueprint of her careful hands. Sometimes I find myself kneeling in that same spot, fingers in the dirt, and I swear I can feel time collapse, all the summers folding into one eternal moment of sun and soil and the smell of basil.

We're all archaeologists of our own lives, constantly excavating. A song plays in a grocery store and suddenly you're seventeen again, sitting in your best friend's car, windows down, singing badly and believing completely that this feeling will last forever. It doesn't, of course. Nothing does. But maybe that's the point – the fleeting nature of things is what makes them pierce us so deeply.

I've been thinking about patterns lately. How we repeat ourselves without knowing it. My mother was terrified of silence; she filled every room with chatter, with television noise, with the clatter of perpetual motion. I swore I'd be different, but last week I caught myself turning on a podcast just to avoid the quiet of an empty apartment. We inherit more than eye color and cheekbones. We inherit fears, habits, the way we hold our coffee cups, the particular sigh we make when we're trying not to cry.

There's a specific loneliness to 3 AM. It's not the same as midnight's drama or 4 AM's resignation. Three in the morning is when the world feels most honest, when you can't lie to yourself anymore about who you are or what you've done. I've made all my biggest decisions at 3 AM – to leave, to stay, to forgive, to finally let go. Something about that hour strips away pretense. Maybe it's evolutionary, some ancient vulnerability from when we were most likely to be eaten by predators. Or maybe it's just that we're too tired to maintain our usual defenses.

The lighthouse keeper's name was Edmund. He had a theory that every seventh wave carries a message. "Count them," he said, pointing out at the grey Atlantic. "Six ordinary waves, then the seventh brings news from somewhere else." I humored him, counting. One, two, three, four, five, six – and then seven would crash against the rocks with what seemed like different intent, fuller somehow, carrying more weight. Probably just a pattern of wind and current, but I counted anyway. Still do, actually, whenever I'm near the ocean.

We underestimate the courage required for ordinary life. Getting up each morning, making breakfast, answering emails, pretending that we're not all just making it up as we go along. There's a kind of heroism in continuing, in choosing to believe that tomorrow might be different, even when all evidence suggests otherwise. My neighbor is ninety-three. She plants bulbs each fall, believing in spring. That's faith. That's resistance against entropy.

I keep a running list of things I want to remember: the way light looked on a particular Tuesday, falling through dust motes in a used bookstore. The exact shade of green the world turns before a thunderstorm. How my father sang in the shower, badly but with complete commitment. The specific weight of a sleeping cat on your lap. The sound of skates on ice, that particular scraping song of winter. These seem like small things, but I've learned that life is mainly composed of small things, threaded together like beads on a string.

Edmund died five years after I met him. They found him at his desk, writing in one of his journals. He'd filled hundreds of them – observations about weather, birds, the moods of the sea. His last entry was incomplete: "The waves today are carrying—" and then nothing. I like to think he finally heard what they were saying.

Sometimes I drive past my childhood home. Other people live there now. They've painted it yellow, which my mother would have hated. But the maple tree we planted when I was seven is enormous now, exactly as we'd imagined it would be when we were kneeling in the dirt, my small hands helping her pat soil around its roots. We plant things we'll never see fully grown. We tell stories we'll never see end. We love people who will continue without us. This is the contract we make with time.

The truth is, I don't know if the sea remembers everything. But I know that we do, in our way. Every person we've been lives inside us like Russian dolls, the child within the teenager within the adult, all of us at once, all of us forever. We carry our ghosts, our might-have-beens, our small victories and large defeats. We carry the words we wished we'd said and the ones we can't take back.

And maybe that's enough. Maybe it's enough to witness, to remember, to count the waves and plant the bulbs and sing badly in the shower. Maybe it's enough to sit in the garden where love once knelt, to feel the sun on our faces and know that we're part of something larger than ourselves, something that remembers.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old woman at the corner table always orders the same thing: black coffee, no sugar, and silence. She brings a notebook—leather-bound, worn at the edges—and writes in it with a fountain pen that bleeds blue ink across cream pages. I've watched her for three months now, every Tuesday and Thursday, 7 AM sharp. Today, she caught me staring.

"You're curious," she said, not a question.

I fumbled with the coffee pot, nearly dropping it. "Sorry, I didn't mean to—"

"Sit." She gestured to the empty chair across from her. The morning rush wouldn't start for another hour. I sat.

"I'm writing down everything I've forgotten," she said, turning the notebook so I could see the pages. They were filled with fragments: *The way rain smelled in 1962. My daughter's first word (was it 'dog' or 'door'?). The recipe for my mother's Sunday sauce. The name of the boy who kissed me behind the gymnasium.*

"But if you've forgotten them, how can you write them down?"

She smiled, and her whole face transformed—suddenly I could see the young woman she'd been, the one who got kissed behind gymnasiums. "I write down the shape of the forgetting. The outline where the memory used to live. Sometimes that's more honest than the memory itself."

I thought about this as I refilled her cup. We all carry these ghost memories, these empty picture frames in our minds. I can't remember my grandfather's voice, but I remember the feeling of sitting beside him on the porch, the creak of his rocking chair keeping time like a metronome. I've forgotten the exact words of the fight that ended my first relationship, but I remember the taste of tears and cheap wine, the way the apartment felt too small and too large at the same time.

The woman—she told me her name was Ruth—came back the next Tuesday with a different notebook. This one was new, burgundy.

"This is for things I haven't forgotten yet," she explained. "But I will. Everything goes eventually."

She wrote while I worked, and I'd glance over occasionally to see her capturing the steam rising from her coffee, the way morning light cut through the blinds, the sound of the espresso machine clearing its throat. She was making an inventory of the present, knowing it would someday be the past.

A month later, Ruth didn't show up. Thursday came and went. The following Tuesday, nothing. I found myself saving her corner table, turning away other customers with elaborate excuses. On the third week, a younger woman came in—Ruth's daughter, though they looked nothing alike.

"She wanted you to have this," she said, handing me the leather notebook, the one full of forgotten things.

"Is she...?"

"Moved to assisted living. Her memory's going faster now. But she remembered you. Said you understood about the forgetting."

I opened the notebook. On the last page, in shaky blue ink: *The young man at the coffee shop who listened. Dark hair, kind eyes. Always overfilled the cup just enough to require a saucer. Tuesdays and Thursdays, 7 AM. What was his name?*

But she had never asked my name. I realized then that she'd already been practicing, already been forgetting me even as she sat across from me, preparing for the day when I would become another outline, another empty frame. She had pre-forgetten me, catalogued the loss before it happened.

I keep the notebook under the counter now. Sometimes, during the slow hours, I read her ghost memories and add my own in the margins. The exact shade of my ex-girlfriend's lipstick. The last thing my father said before the surgery. The name of my childhood dog (was it Buster or Baxter?). 

There's something beautiful about acknowledging the forgetting, about giving it space and shape. We spend so much time trying to hold on, to preserve, to remember. But maybe Ruth had it right. Maybe the forgetting is just as important as the remembering. Maybe it's the empty spaces that define us as much as the filled ones.

Last week, a young couple sat at Ruth's corner table. They were clearly on a first date, nervous and eager, checking their phones when the conversation lulled. I wanted to tell them to put the phones away, to memorize each other's faces, the way they laughed, the way their hands almost touched but didn't. I wanted to tell them that someday they might forget this moment, and that's okay. The forgetting doesn't diminish the living.

Instead, I brought them their coffee and watched as they began creating memories they would someday lose, writing their own story in invisible ink, every moment both permanent and passing, like steam rising from a cup, like blue ink bleeding into cream paper, like an old woman who orders the same thing until she doesn't, who remembers everything until she can't, who teaches a young barista that forgetting is just another kind of remembering, turned inside out.

The notebook gains entries. The coffee keeps brewing. The corner table waits.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old woman at the farmers market sells honey in mason jars with handwritten labels, and every Saturday she tells me the same story about her bees. How they know her footsteps. How they dance their directions to the wildflower fields beyond the highway. Today she adds something new: "They're dreaming of winter already. I can tell by how they fly—a little slower, a little heavier, like they're carrying next season's sleep in their wings."

I buy three jars because her hands shake now when she makes change, and I want her to go home early, rest those swollen knuckles. But really, I buy them because I need to believe in something as simple as bees knowing footsteps.

The subway home is delayed. Signal problems, they announce, which means nothing and everything. A mariachi band works through the stopped cars, accordion breathing between stations, and a child drops her ice cream cone, watches it roll across the dirty floor with such profound betrayal in her eyes that her mother buys her another one from the platform vendor even though it's almost dinner time. Small rebellions against Tuesday afternoon tragedy.

I've been thinking about time lately—how it moves like honey when you're young, then water, then wind. My nephew asked me last week what it feels like to be old. I'm only thirty-six, but to him, I might as well be ancient. I told him it feels like being young but with more ghosts. He didn't understand. How could he? His ghosts haven't been born yet.

There's a particular quality of light that only exists at 4:17 PM in October, when the sun hits the kitchen window and turns everything gold and dusty, like the inside of an antique shop. My grandmother's china catches it, the plates I never use but can't give away, and for a moment I can smell her cigarettes and lavender perfume, hear her humming off-key while washing dishes. She's been gone twelve years. The plates haven't moved.

Someone on the internet wrote that we die twice—once when we stop breathing, and again when someone says our name for the last time. But I think we die a little bit every day, shedding old selves like snake skin, leaving behind versions of ourselves in places we'll never return to. The girl who cried over failed algebra. The woman who said yes to the wrong ring. The person who stood at the intersection of Broadway and 42nd, overwhelmed by the noise and possibility, wondering if this was what happiness looked like or just its loud cousin.

The honey woman's bees are already dreaming of winter. What do bees dream? Hexagons and heat, maybe. The democracy of the swarm. Their queen's pheromones like a song only they can hear. Or maybe they dream of being anything but bees—eagles or whales or humans who buy honey in mason jars and pretend to understand stories about insects carrying seasons in their wings.

My neighbor is learning violin. She's terrible, but she practices every evening at seven, and there's something hopeful about those screeling notes floating through the walls. She's seventy-three, started last month. "Why now?" I asked her in the hallway. "Why not?" she said, and I couldn't think of a single good answer.

This is what I know: The world is ending and beginning every second. Children are being born while others take their last breath. Someone is falling in love in a coffee shop while someone else signs divorce papers. The Arctic is melting. Somewhere, a seed that's been dormant for decades suddenly decides to grow. These things are equally true and equally important, and if you think about it too much, you'll never get out of bed.

But you do get out of bed. You buy honey. You listen to bad violin. You save voice messages from people you love, just in case. You water the plants even though you suspect they're already dead. You write thank-you notes. You learn the names of your barista's cats. You hold the door. You overtip. You text "thinking of you" and mean it. You make soup from scratch. You call your mother.

The light is fading now, that golden 4:17 moment passing into regular evening. Rush hour is starting. People are hurrying home to their own kitchens, their own ghosts, their own china they never use. Tomorrow the honey woman will be at the market again if it doesn't rain, telling someone else about her bees, how they know her footsteps, how they're already dreaming of winter.

Maybe that's enough—to be known by bees, to carry seasons in our wings, to practice violin badly at seventy-three. To buy three jars of honey from an old woman with swollen knuckles and believe, really believe, that somewhere in those amber jars is the distilled essence of ten thousand flowers, the work of creatures who dream in hexagons, the promise that even when winter comes, there will be sweetness stored away, waiting.

The mariachi music echoes in the subway tunnel. The child with her new ice cream smiles. We move forward through the signal problems, toward our various homes, carrying our Tuesdays like careful cargo, like bees heavy with tomorrow's sleep.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old man at the coffee shop always ordered the same thing: black coffee, no sugar, and a plain croissant. Every morning at 7:15, like clockwork. I started noticing him during my third week of unemployment, when my days had become an endless scroll through job listings and my savings account dwindled like ice in summer.

He sat by the window, the one with the crack that the owner had covered with clear tape that caught the light in rainbow fragments. His hands, spotted with age and trembling slightly, would wrap around the white ceramic mug as if drawing warmth from more than just the coffee. I wondered what he thought about, staring out at the street where people rushed past in their morning urgency, their faces set in that particular expression of needing to be somewhere else.

One Tuesday, his chair was empty.

Wednesday too.

By Thursday, I asked the barista—a girl with purple hair and a nose ring who always drew hearts in my foam despite my requests for regular coffee. She shrugged. "Maybe he's sick. Or visiting family."

But something felt wrong. You develop a sense for disruption when your own life has been disrupted. You notice when patterns break.

I found myself walking past his usual spot even when I ordered to-go. The table looked larger without him there, accusatory somehow, like a stage missing its actor. The rainbow light from the cracked window fell across the empty wooden surface, illuminating nothing.

On Friday, I decided to wait. I took his table—it felt like trespassing—and ordered black coffee and a plain croissant. The coffee was bitter, the croissant dry. How had he done this every day? The street outside moved in its usual choreography: a woman walking three small dogs that tangled their leashes, a delivery truck blocking the bike lane, tourists consulting their phones with confused expressions.

"You're in George's spot."

I looked up. A woman, perhaps seventy, stood beside the table. She wore a lavender coat despite the warm weather and carried a purse that looked older than me.

"George?"

"The man who sits here." She gestured at the chair. "Every morning for the past six years, since Martha died."

"Do you know where he is?"

She sat down without invitation, placing her purse carefully on the table. "Hospital. Fell in his apartment on Monday evening. Hip surgery." She studied me with sharp blue eyes. "You've been watching him."

It wasn't a question, but I nodded anyway. "I've been coming here a lot lately. Job hunting. He was just... always here."

"Routine," she said. "It's what keeps us anchored when everything else drifts away. George lost Martha, lost his purpose. But he had this—coffee at 7:15, crossword puzzle at 7:45, walk through the park at 8:30."

"How do you know his schedule?"

She smiled, a secret tucked in the corners of her mouth. "I have my own routines. We orbit each other, all of us regulars. The man with the laptop in the corner—divorced, sees his kids on weekends. The woman who brings her own tea bag—caring for her mother with dementia. You—recently unemployed, probably in tech from the look of your laptop bag, struggling with the sudden absence of structure."

I felt exposed, read like a book I didn't know I'd opened.

"Will he be okay? George?"

"Define okay." She picked up my untouched croissant, tore off a piece, chewed thoughtfully. "He'll heal, probably. Physical therapy. Walker for a while. But will he return to this exact spot at 7:15? Continue his crossword? Hard to say. Sometimes a break in routine is just a pause. Sometimes it's an ending."

She stood, leaving the croissant decimated on the plate. "You could visit him. Room 412 at Mercy General. He won't expect it, but that's not always a bad thing."

After she left, I sat there until my coffee went cold, watching the street, understanding finally what George might have seen. Not just people passing by, but life itself—constant, indifferent, beautiful in its relentlessness. The crack in the window wasn't a flaw; it was a prism, breaking plain light into something spectacular.

That afternoon, I bought a newspaper and learned to do crossword puzzles. Four across: "Daily practice." Eight letters. 

ROUTINE.

At the hospital, George looked smaller in the white bed, but his eyes were the same—alert, watching. I placed the newspaper on his bedside table.

"Thought you might need this. I've been keeping your table warm."

He didn't speak for a moment, then: "Martha always said strangers are just friends you haven't shared coffee with yet."

"Smart woman."

"The smartest."

We sat in silence, but it was comfortable, like we'd done this before. Outside his window, the city continued its patterns—ambulances arriving, visitors leaving, life persisting in its mysterious ways. I thought about orbits, about anchors, about the small rituals that keep us tethered to meaning when meaning seems lost.

"Same time tomorrow?" I asked.

"7:15," he confirmed. "Though the coffee here is terrible."

"I'll bring some from the shop."

He smiled then, and I understood that sometimes unemployment isn't just about losing a job—it's about finding the space to notice what was always there, waiting to matter. The rainbow light through cracked windows. The empty chairs that need filling. The strangers who are just friends you haven't shared coffee with yet.

Tomorrow, 7:15. Black coffee, no sugar. A new routine begins.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old woman at the farmers market sells honey in mason jars with handwritten labels, and every Saturday she tells me the same story about her bees. How they know her footsteps. How they dance in figure-eights to share the location of wildflower meadows. How once, during a thunderstorm, she found them all huddled together in the hive, humming at a frequency she could feel in her bones.

I don't know if I believe her, but I buy the honey anyway.

There's something about repetition that creates its own kind of truth. The way my father cleared his throat before saying something important. The way cats always find the one patch of sunlight on the floor. The way everyone claims their grandmother made the best version of whatever food they're eating.

Memory is unreliable, they say, but what else do we have? I remember the taste of tap water from garden hoses in summer, metallic and warm and somehow perfect. I remember my first dog's eyes going cloudy, how he still wagged his tail when he heard my voice. I remember the exact shade of blue nail polish my mother wore the day she taught me to parallel park, though I can't remember what we talked about.

Sometimes I make lists of things I'm afraid I'll forget:
- The sound of VHS tapes rewinding
- Phone numbers we used to memorize
- How to fold a paper fortune teller
- The smell of libraries before they all started serving coffee
- Waiting for photos to develop, the anticipation of not knowing

My neighbor grows tomatoes in his backyard. He's eighty-three and waters them every evening at 6:15, wearing the same canvas hat. Last week, he gave me a bag of Cherokee Purples, these ugly, beautiful things that looked almost bruised. "They don't ship well," he said, "so you won't find them in stores." They tasted like summer concentrated, like the platonic ideal of tomato. I ate them with just salt, standing over the sink.

We're losing things all the time. Not just species and glaciers and languages, though yes, those too. We're losing the ability to be bored. To not know something immediately. To be unreachable. My niece doesn't understand why we can't just pause live TV, why some movies aren't available to stream, why she can't video call her friend who's camping.

But we're gaining things too, I suppose. My mother video-tours me through her garden from three thousand miles away. I can hear any song I want, instantly. These words I'm writing could be read by someone in Mumbai or Stockholm or a small town in Kansas I've never heard of.

Still, I miss handwriting. Not romantically, but actually miss it. The way your hand would cramp during essay exams. The specific anxiety of running out of ink. The way you could tell someone's mood by their penmanship. I have a box of letters from an old friend who died five years ago. Her handwriting got smaller as she got sicker, as if she was trying to fit more words onto each page, racing against time.

The honey woman's story changes slightly each week, though she doesn't seem to notice. Sometimes the storm is in June, sometimes August. Sometimes the bees hum in B-flat, sometimes C. But the heart of it remains: the bees knew she would keep them safe, and so they sang.

I've started keeping my own bees, though I haven't told her. They're nothing special—just regular honeybees in a white box in my backyard. I don't know their language yet. They don't recognize my footsteps. But yesterday, I sat near the hive for an hour, just listening. They do hum. It's not musical, exactly, but it's not not musical either. It's the sound of ten thousand small bodies doing the work of living.

There's a certain kind of loneliness that comes from being the only one who remembers something. The last speaker of a language. The only survivor of an accident. The sole keeper of a family recipe. But there's also a kind of power in it, a terrible responsibility. You become the bridge between what was and what is.

My grandmother used to make these cookies—brown butter with cardamom and rose water. I watched her make them dozens of times but never wrote down the recipe. She cooked by feel, by smell, by the way the dough pulled away from her fingers. After she died, I spent years trying to recreate them. I came close once, close enough that I cried into the mixing bowl, but it was never quite right. The flavor was there but not the feeling. The taste but not the time.

Now I make my own version. Different, but related. A descendant rather than a replica. I write down everything: measurements, temperatures, the exact color the butter should turn. Someday someone might want to know.

The honey woman won't be at the farmers market forever. Her hands shake now when she counts change. She's started bringing her daughter to help carry the jars. But for now, every Saturday, she tells me about the storm, about the humming, about the bees who know her footsteps.

And every Saturday, I listen like it's the first time.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The old woman at the corner table orders the same thing every morning: black coffee, no sugar, and silence. I've been serving her for three months now, and we've never exchanged more than seven words. "The usual?" I ask. "Please," she responds. Sometimes "thank you" when I set the cup down. The ceramic clinks against the saucer like a tiny bell.

Today she's wearing purple gloves, which strikes me as odd because it's July and the air conditioning in here barely works. They're velvet, I think, or something that wants to be velvet. She turns the pages of her newspaper with them on, leaving small smudges on the edges. I wonder if the ink stains the fabric.

The coffee shop is mostly empty at 6 AM. Just her, me, and Marcus who's pretending to write a screenplay on his laptop but is actually scrolling through his ex-girlfriend's Instagram. I know because I can see the reflection in the window behind him. Her new boyfriend has a boat. Marcus doesn't have a boat. This seems to matter immensely to him.

The espresso machine hisses. I pull shots for no one, just to keep my hands busy. The movements are automatic now—tamp, twist, pull, steam. My body knows this dance better than any other. Sometimes I catch myself making these motions in my sleep, my girlfriend tells me. Phantom lattes.

The woman in purple gloves stands up. This is unusual. She normally stays until seven-fifteen, precise as clockwork. But it's only six-thirty. She walks to the counter, and I notice she's limping slightly. New hip, maybe. My grandmother walked like that after hers.

"I want to tell you something," she says.

My hand hovers over the register, uncertain. "Okay."

"Forty-three years ago, I killed a man in this building."

The milk steamer chooses this moment to screech. Marcus looks up from his phone. The woman doesn't flinch.

"It was self-defense," she continues, as if discussing the weather. "This wasn't a coffee shop then. It was a print shop. My husband's print shop. He was drunk again, angrier than usual. Had a wrench in his hand."

I don't know what to say, so I say nothing. This seems right somehow.

"I pushed him. He fell backwards, hit his head on the corner of the printing press. Right about—" she points to where we keep the bag of coffee beans "—there."

Marcus is definitely listening now, laptop forgotten.

"Nobody asked questions. He was known for drinking, for falling. The police came, wrote it down as an accident. Case closed. I sold the shop, moved away for thirty years. Then I came back, and it was this." She gestures around at the exposed brick, the Edison bulbs, the chalkboard menu.

"Why are you telling me?" I ask.

She pulls off one purple glove, then the other. Her hands are spotted with age, but steady. "Because you're the only person in this town who doesn't try to fill my silence with noise. Everyone else wants to chat, to know my story, to be friendly. You just let me be. That's worth something."

She places a fifty-dollar bill on the counter. "For the coffee. All of it. These three months."

"That's too much—"

"It's not enough." She turns to leave, then stops. "Do you believe in ghosts?"

"I don't know."

"Good answer. I don't either. But sometimes, early in the morning, I hear the printing press. That's why I come here. To listen. To make sure it's really gone."

She walks out, leaving the purple gloves on the counter. I pick them up. They're softer than I expected, and warm. There's a label inside: "Property of St. Mary's Hospital." I Google it later. St. Mary's closed in 1981.

Marcus comes to the counter after she leaves. "That was intense," he says.

"Yeah."

"You think it's true?"

I look at the spot she pointed to. There's a dark stain on the floor I've never noticed before. Could be coffee. Could be anything. "Does it matter?"

He considers this, then returns to his laptop. To the boats and the ex-girlfriend and the life he's not living.

I keep the gloves under the counter. She doesn't come back the next day, or the next. On the third day, I check the obituaries. Nothing. On the fourth day, I stop checking.

Two weeks later, a young woman comes in at 6 AM. Orders black coffee, no sugar. Sits at the corner table. Opens a book. Doesn't try to make conversation. I bring her the coffee and she nods, almost imperceptibly.

The gloves are still under the counter. Sometimes I touch them, just to make sure they're real. They always are. Soft and purple and impossible, like the best secrets.

The espresso machine hisses. Marcus types nothing on his laptop. The morning slides forward into afternoon, and I think about ghosts, about the sounds old buildings make, about the stories we tell strangers when we can't bear to tell them to ourselves.

Somewhere, maybe, an old woman is listening to a printing press that doesn't exist. Or maybe she never existed either. Maybe I invented her out of the need for mystery, for something beyond the daily grind of coffee grounds.

But the gloves are real. I'm holding them now. They smell like lavender and time.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
## The Strange Comfort of Uncertainty

I've been thinking lately about the peculiar relationship we have with not knowing things. There's something both terrifying and oddly liberating about uncertainty that seems to define so much of conscious experience. We spend enormous amounts of energy trying to predict, control, and understand, yet the moments that often matter most are the ones we never see coming.

Consider how we wake up each morning. The alarm goes off—or doesn't—and consciousness reassembles itself from whatever strange dream logic held us moments before. For those first few seconds, we don't quite know where we are, what day it is, or what awaits us. There's a brief window where anything could be true, where the world hasn't yet solidified into its familiar patterns. Most of us rush through this liminal space, eager to ground ourselves in the known. But what if we lingered there a little longer?

I find myself fascinated by these threshold moments. The pause before opening an email that could change everything. The silence after asking someone an important question. The moment before the doctor walks back into the room with test results. These are pockets of pure potential, where multiple futures exist simultaneously until reality collapses into just one.

We've built entire civilizations around the project of reducing uncertainty. Weather forecasts, insurance policies, retirement plans, wedding vows—all attempts to nail down the future, to make it behave. Yet the universe seems to have a sense of humor about our efforts. The most carefully laid plans are undone by a chance encounter, a delayed flight, a conversation overheard in a coffee shop. And thank goodness for that. Imagine how insufferably boring life would be if everything went according to plan.

The pandemic, of course, was uncertainty's grand demonstration of power. All our careful scheduling, our five-year plans, our assumptions about how life was supposed to unfold—swept away in a matter of weeks. We learned that "normal" was just a story we'd been telling ourselves, a collective agreement that could be revoked at any time. Some people found this terrifying. Others found it strangely freeing, as if they'd been given permission to admit they'd never really known what they were doing anyway.

There's a certain type of person who claims to thrive on uncertainty, who proudly declares themselves spontaneous and adaptable. But I wonder if what they really mean is they've learned to surf small waves of uncertainty while still counting on the ocean to behave more or less predictably. True uncertainty—the kind where you don't even know what you don't know—is something else entirely. It's not jumping out of a plane with a parachute; it's not being sure if gravity will work the same way when you land.

Scientists understand this better than most. The history of science is largely a chronicle of being wrong in increasingly sophisticated ways. We thought the Earth was flat, then round, then an oblate spheroid. We thought atoms were indivisible, then discovered subatomic particles, then quantum mechanics revealed a reality so strange that even Einstein struggled to accept it. Each discovery doesn't so much answer questions as reveal how poorly we'd been asking them in the first place.

And yet, there's something beautiful about the scientific method's embrace of provisional knowledge. Every finding ends with an implicit "as far as we know" and "for now." It's a kind of intellectual humility that acknowledges uncertainty as a feature, not a bug. The most exciting phrase in science isn't "Eureka!" but "That's weird..."

In our personal lives, we're often less graceful about not knowing. We construct elaborate narratives about why things happened the way they did, as if we could reverse-engineer meaning from randomness. That relationship failed because of this, that job didn't work out because of that. We need these stories—they're how we make sense of our lives. But sometimes I wonder if we're like ancient astronomers drawing constellations, finding patterns in scattered stars that have nothing to do with each other except their position relative to our particular viewpoint.

Memory itself is a kind of uncertainty disguised as certainty. We remember events not as they were but as we've reconstructed them, each recollection slightly different from the last. The story of our first kiss, our worst day, our greatest triumph—all of these shift and evolve each time we recall them. We're constantly rewriting our own histories, usually without realizing it. The person you were ten years ago might not recognize the story you now tell about them.
